subject: Social Studies
description: Create a timeline of noteworthy events to better understand the historical and contemporary events of the local community or region.
description_plain: Your child can make a timeline to show important events in the community's past and present.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use primary and secondary sources, including conducting interviews, to research the history of the local community or region.
description_plain: Your child can use books, interviews, and other sources to research the history of their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Continuity and Change (H.CH)
description_plain: Your child learns how things have changed or stayed the same over time in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: History
description_plain: Your child learns about the history of their community and region.

subject: Social Studies
description: With prompting and support, ask and answer questions about buying, selling, or trading something and explain how people make choices about the things they need and want.
description_plain: With help, your child can ask and answer questions about buying, selling, or trading things, and explain how people decide what they need or want.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate examples of specialization and economic interdependence in the local community.
description_plain: Your child can find examples of people in the community doing special jobs and depending on each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Specialization, Trade, and Interdependence (E.ST)
description_plain: Your child understands how people do different jobs, trade, and rely on each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain opportunity cost and how it influences buying decisions.
description_plain: Your child can explain that choosing one thing often means giving up something else, and how this affects what people buy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of advertising and peer pressure in decision-making.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ads and friends can influence what people choose to buy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Incentives, Choice, and Consumer Behavior (E.IC)
description_plain: Your child understands how people make choices about spending money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effect of government and business decisions on personal and community resources.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions made by the government or businesses affect what people and the community have.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the relationship between supply and demand in setting the price of goods and services in the marketplace.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the amount of goods and how much people want them affects prices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Micro and Macro Economics (E.MI)
description_plain: Your child learns about how money, goods, and services work in the community and beyond.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose of taxes in supporting examples of public goods.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people pay taxes and how taxes help pay for things everyone uses, like parks or schools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Consider the effect of individual financial decisions on personal, community, regional, and world resources.
description_plain: Your child can think about how their money choices affect themselves, their community, and even the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Earning, Saving, and Spending (E.ES)
description_plain: Your child understands how people earn, save, and spend money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Economics
description_plain: Your child learns about how people make choices with money and resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and analyze Oregon’s natural resources and describe how people in Oregon and other parts of the world use them.
description_plain: Your child can name Oregon’s natural resources and explain how people in Oregon and around the world use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare how the physical and human geography of different Oregon regions affects the attributes of local communities.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the land and people in different parts of Oregon shape what local communities are like.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human Environmental Interaction (G.HE)
description_plain: Your child understands how people and the environment affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the diverse aspects of culture represented in a community, such as individuals, events, songs, symbols, and celebrations of community.
description_plain: Your child can compare different cultures in a community by looking at people, events, songs, symbols, and celebrations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human Interaction and Interconnection (G.HI)
description_plain: Your child understands how people interact and connect with each other in communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the causes behind global migration, distinguishing between voluntary relocation and forced displacement.
description_plain: Your child can find out why people move to new places, and tell the difference between moving by choice and being forced to move.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate multiple theories on the populating and human movement in North and South America using archaeological evidence for the origins of people in North America, including in the Pacific Northwest.
description_plain: Your child can look at evidence to learn how people first came to North and South America, including the Pacific Northwest.

subject: Social Studies
description: Migration and Movement (G.MM)
description_plain: Your child understands why and how people move from one place to another.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use lines of latitude and longitude on multiple types of maps, globes, and images to locate and describe tribal lands, environmental regions, and counties of Oregon and the greater Pacific Northwest.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and globes to find places in Oregon and the Pacific Northwest using latitude and longitude.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between physical, political, cultural, and thematic maps of Oregon and the United States.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between different kinds of maps, like maps showing land, borders, cultures, or special topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geographic Reasoning (G.GR)
description_plain: Your child learns how to use maps and other tools to understand places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geography
description_plain: Your child studies the land, people, and places around them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a local public issue and describe ways individuals and groups can engage with decision-makers to have influence in the civic life of their communities.
description_plain: Your child can name a local problem and describe ways people can work with leaders to help make decisions in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Engagement (C.CE)
description_plain: Your child understands how people can get involved and help make decisions in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the democratic principle of a smaller voting group (the minority) having rights that the larger voting group (the majority) cannot take away.
description_plain: Your child can explain that even if most people vote one way, the rights of the smaller group must still be protected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Democratic Principles (C.DP)
description_plain: Your child learns about the basic rules and ideas of democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the responsibilities of people in their community and state.
description_plain: Your child can describe what people are expected to do in their community and state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how a community relies on active civic participation and identify opportunities for student participation in local and regional issues.
description_plain: Your child can explain why it’s important for people to take part in their community and find ways they can help with local issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize that people's identities and individual experiences can lead to different interpretations of situations and events.
description_plain: Your child understands that people’s backgrounds and experiences can make them see things differently.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the differences between allies and bystanders exploring how individuals can promote democratic values such as liberty, equality, and justice when they see someone targeted for who they are.
description_plain: Your child can explain the difference between helping someone and just watching, and how people can stand up for fairness when someone is treated badly.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identity, Roles, & Responsibilities (C.IR)
description_plain: Your child learns about who they are, their roles, and what they are responsible for in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify state offices, leaders, and their functions.
description_plain: Your child can name state leaders and explain what their jobs are.

subject: Social Studies
description: Political Institutions (C.PI)
description_plain: Your child understands what government offices and leaders do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civics
description_plain: Your child learns about how communities are run and how people work together to make rules and solve problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify various conflicts within Oregon, including acts of displacement, cultural and linguistic loss, violence, economic competition, political disagreements, boundary disputes, and cultural disparities across different geographical regions and demographic groups.
description_plain: Your child can name different conflicts in Oregon’s history, like fights over land, culture, language, money, politics, and boundaries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain how the legacy of colonialism and discrimination based on race, gender, economic, and social group identity created and continues to affect the history, growth, and current experience of residents of Oregon.
description_plain: Your child can explain how unfair treatment based on race, gender, or social group has shaped Oregon’s history and still affects people today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the history, religion, languages, and cultural practices of the Indigenous Tribes of Oregon and examples of resistance and resilience to immigration and settlement by the United States.
description_plain: Your child can describe the history, beliefs, languages, and traditions of Oregon’s Indigenous Tribes and how they have stood up to newcomers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communities and Pluralism (H.CP)
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups live together and how communities are made up of many kinds of people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use multiple sources to explore the Oregon Trail’s significance in shaping Oregon history, examining its role in westward expansion, cultural interactions, and settlement patterns.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to learn why the Oregon Trail was important for Oregon’s history, including how it changed where people lived and how cultures met.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how changes in Oregon’s agricultural, industrial, political, and business development over time affect people of the state including traditionally underrepresented groups.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how changes in farming, business, and politics in Oregon have affected different groups of people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Cause and Effect (H.CE)
description_plain: Your child learns how events and changes affect people and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of Indigenous resistance to the expansion of non-Indigenous settlers into Oregon lands in the causes and results of the battles and wars in Oregon Territory and 19th century Oregon.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how Indigenous people in Oregon resisted settlers and what happened because of those conflicts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Conflict and Cooperation (H.CC)
description_plain: Your child understands how groups sometimes work together and sometimes have conflicts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the role of “Manifest Destiny” and racial prejudice in shaping Oregon’s transition from “Oregon Country” to statehood. Include the legal, political, and cultural factors causing marginalization and attempts to promote inclusivity.
description_plain: Your child can find out how ideas like 'Manifest Destiny' and prejudice affected Oregon becoming a state, including laws and actions that excluded some people and efforts to include everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and examine the Indigenous sovereign people of Oregon prior to and during waves of exploration and settlement from Europe and the United States. Students should engage with lessons provided in Tribal History/Shared History to identify the first nine people groups of Oregon.
description_plain: Your child can identify Oregon’s first Indigenous groups and learn about their lives before and during the arrival of explorers and settlers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Continuity and Change (H.CH)
description_plain: Your child learns about what has stayed the same and what has changed over time in Oregon’s history.

subject: Social Studies
description: History
description_plain: Your child studies important events and people from the past, especially in Oregon.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how trade leads to increasing economic interdependence.
description_plain: Your child can explain how trading with others means people and places depend on each other for goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Specialization, Trade, Interdependence (E.ST)
description_plain: Your child understands that people often focus on making certain things (specialization), trade with others, and rely on each other to get what they need.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate understanding of safe internet practices by applying strategies to protect personal information.
description_plain: Your child can show how to stay safe online by using ways to protect their personal information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze different buying choices and opportunity costs.
description_plain: Your child can look at different things to buy and think about what they might have to give up when making a choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the use of targeted marketing in creating demand for consumer products.
description_plain: Your child can spot how ads and marketing try to make people want to buy certain products.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate understanding of needs and wants using a budget.
description_plain: Your child can use a budget to show the difference between things they need and things they want.

subject: Social Studies
description: Incentives, Choice, and Consumer Behavior (E.IC)
description_plain: Your child understands that rewards and choices affect how people decide what to buy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the relationship between the supply and demand of goods produced in Oregon.
description_plain: Your child can explore how the amount of goods made in Oregon and how much people want them affects prices and availability.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of producers, consumers, products, and labor in economic markets.
description_plain: Your child can explain what producers, consumers, products, and workers do in the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Micro and Macro Economics (E.MI)
description_plain: Your child understands how small and large parts of the economy work together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe examples of government-provided goods and services funded with taxes.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of things like roads, schools, or parks that the government provides using tax money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the difference between wages, salaries, commissions, and tips.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between ways people get paid, like wages, salaries, commissions, and tips.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the consequences of power and privilege on issues associated with poverty, income, and wealth accumulation.
description_plain: Your child can look at how having more power or money can affect issues like poverty and wealth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Earning, Saving, and Spending (E.ES)
description_plain: Your child understands how people earn, save, and spend money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Economics
description_plain: Your child learns about how money, jobs, and resources work in our world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the Social Science Tribal History Shared History lessons to explore the relationship of the Nine federally recognized Tribes in Oregon with physical and human geography.
description_plain: Your child can use lessons about Oregon's nine Tribes to learn how they connect with the land and people around them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the effects on environmental stability and sustainability of technologies and policies such as dams, wind turbines, and transportation in shaping Oregon's physical and human geography.
description_plain: Your child can identify how things like dams, wind turbines, and transportation affect Oregon’s environment and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human Environmental Interaction (G.HE)
description_plain: Your child understands how people and the environment affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess how physical geography and changing environmental factors affect land use and shape the cultural characteristics of a society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the land and environment change how people use land and shape their culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human Interaction and Interconnection (G.HI)
description_plain: Your child understands how people interact with each other and their surroundings.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child can explain how Oregon’s Tribes and different immigrant groups have helped make Oregon’s culture diverse today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Migration and Movement (G.MM)
description_plain: Your child understands how and why people move and settle in new places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast tribal, colonial, historical, and contemporary maps to understand Oregon's history.
description_plain: Your child can compare old and new maps to learn about Oregon’s history.

subject: Social Studies
description: On political and physical maps of North America, locate significant sites in the United States, Mexico, Canada, Oregon, Nevada, Alaska, Hawai`i, Washington, Idaho, and California.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child can read or make maps of different US regions using a scale, compass, and key to show cities and land features.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geographic Reasoning (G.GR)
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps and other tools to learn about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geography
description_plain: Your child learns about places, people, and the land around them.

description_plain: Your child can describe why it’s important for people to get involved in government, like voting or helping change laws in Oregon.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Engagement (C.CE)
description_plain: Your child understands how people can take part in their community and government.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child can find examples in Oregon’s Constitution that talk about fairness, equality, or stopping discrimination.

subject: Social Studies
description: Democratic Principles (C.DP)
description_plain: Your child understands the basic ideas of fairness and equality in government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine how identity shapes perspectives about a local or state issue.
description_plain: Your child can look at how who we are can affect how we see local or state issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identity, Roles, & Responsibilities (C.IR)
description_plain: Your child understands how identity shapes our roles and responsibilities in the community.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the creation of the Oregon constitution and identify its key components. Note: Teachers may choose the sections of the Constitution that are most accessible and relevant to their students.
description_plain: Your child can learn how Oregon’s Constitution was made and point out its main parts.

description: Political Institutions (C.PI)
description_plain: Your child understands how governments are organized and how rules are made.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civics
description_plain: Your child learns about how government and citizenship work.

description_plain: Your child can explain how different Indigenous peoples in North America lived and understood the world before Europeans arrived.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the ideas and actions of individuals and groups resisting enslavement, indigenous genocide, and denial of equality and justice with connections to present-day issues.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people and groups fought against slavery, harm to Indigenous peoples, and unfair treatment, and connect these actions to issues we see today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communities and Pluralism (H.CP)
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups of people live together and share ideas in communities.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child can tell about the leaders and daily lives of the United States' founders, including how they interacted with Indigenous peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Cause and Effect (H.CE)
description_plain: Your child understands how one event can cause another and how to look for reasons and results in history.

description_plain: Your child can explain how attempts to erase Indigenous cultures, including forced changes and violence, affected Native peoples in the United States.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child can describe how working together or fighting between Indigenous peoples and European settlers changed life in America between 1500 and 1776.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine how the decisions of those in power affected those with less political/economic power in past and current movements for equality, freedom, and justice with connections to the present day.
description_plain: Your child can explain how decisions made by people in power have affected those with less power, both in the past and today, especially in movements for fairness and justice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Conflict and Cooperation (H.CC)
description_plain: Your child understands how groups sometimes work together and sometimes have conflicts, and how this shapes history.

description_plain: Your child can use maps and other tools to show where Europeans explored and settled in North America and the Caribbean, and explain why they came and what happened to the Indigenous peoples.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child can explain how enslaving Indigenous peoples and Africans helped build the colonies and the United States, how slavery ended in the North, and how it spread to the West.

subject: Social Studies
description: Continuity and Change (H.CH)
description_plain: Your child understands what has stayed the same and what has changed over time in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: History
description_plain: Your child learns about important events and people from the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why cultures and civilizations choose to specialize in producing selected goods or services.
description_plain: Your child can explain why groups or countries focus on making certain products or services instead of others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Specialization, Trade, Interdependence (E.ST)
description_plain: Your child understands how people and countries trade with each other and depend on one another for goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss how life circumstances and experiences can cause people to differ in their values and attitudes about saving and their ability to save.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how people's backgrounds and experiences affect how and why they save money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how incentives and opportunity costs affect decision-making.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rewards and trade-offs influence the choices people make.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze career choices with consideration of necessary qualifications, income potential, and time commitment.
description_plain: Your child can think about different jobs by looking at what skills are needed, how much money they can make, and how much time each job takes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Incentives, Choice, and Consumer Behavior (E.IC)
description_plain: Your child understands why people make certain choices when buying things and how rewards or costs affect those choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the significance of the slave trade among and between the Americas, Europe, Asia, and Africa.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the slave trade between the Americas, Europe, Asia, and Africa was important in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the United States’ development from a mercantilist to a market economy.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the United States changed from a system where the government controlled trade to one where people could buy and sell freely.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe why the government collects taxes and what goods and services it provides society.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the government collects taxes and what services and goods it provides with that money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the relationship between supply and demand.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the amount of something available and how much people want it affects prices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Micro and Macro Economics (E.MI)
description_plain: Your child understands how the economy works, both for individuals and for the whole country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Estimate income that a youth-operated business can earn.

description: Map out a savings and budget plan designed to achieve a future purchase objective.
description_plain: Your child can make a plan to save money and budget for something they want to buy in the future.

subject: Social Studies
description: Earning, Saving, and Spending (E.ES)
description_plain: Your child understands how to earn, save, and spend money wisely.

subject: Social Studies
description: Economics
description_plain: Your child learns about how people make, use, and share resources and money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how technological developments, societal decisions, and personal practices affect sustainability in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain how technology, community choices, and personal habits affect the environment in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how physical, human, and political features influence events, movements, and adaptation to the environment.
description_plain: Your child can describe how natural features, people, and government decisions affect events and how people adapt to their surroundings.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human Environmental Interaction (G.HE)
description_plain: Your child understands how people and the environment affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the effects of exploration, conquest, exploitation, and colonial settlement on Indigenous Tribes in at least two geographic regions.
description_plain: Your child can find out how exploration and settlement by Europeans changed the lives of Indigenous Tribes in at least two different areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Trace on a map Indigenous Tribes' encounters with Europeans in North America and the Caribbean Islands in the 15th through the 18th centuries.
description_plain: Your child can use a map to show where Indigenous Tribes met Europeans in North America and the Caribbean between the 1400s and 1700s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human Interaction and Interconnection (G.HI)
description_plain: Your child understands how people interact and connect with each other across different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the causes behind domestic and global migration, distinguishing between voluntary relocation and forced displacement, including scenarios like refugees, individuals compelled to leave their homeland and those enslaved.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people move within a country or to other countries, and tell the difference between moving by choice and being forced to move, such as refugees or enslaved people.

description: Identify and analyze the implications and ramifications for Native American Tribes of the movement of people, goods, ideas, and cultural patterns to what became the United States, considering past, present, and future trends.

description_plain: Your child understands why people move from one place to another and how this affects everyone.

description: Locate and examine significant physical geographic features of the thirteen British colonies that became the United States.

description: Locate states, capital cities, and important geographic features on a United States map.

subject: Social Studies

description: Geography

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Identity, Roles, & Responsibilities (C.IR)

description_plain: Your child can compare how the British monarchy, American colonies, and Indigenous Tribes governed and interacted with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Political Institutions (C.PI)

subject: Social Studies
description: Civics
description_plain: Your child learns about how government works and how people can be good citizens.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child understands how some things change and some things stay the same over time in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: History
description_plain: Your child is learning about important events and people from the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how regional specialization created economic interdependence among the regions of the United States between 1783-1865.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different parts of the United States depended on each other for goods and services between 1783 and 1865.

subject: Social Studies
description: Specialization, Trade, and Interdependence (E.ST)
description_plain: Your child understands how people and regions specialize in certain jobs or products and trade with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess the effect of business growth and expansion on the allocation of resources and economic incentives.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the growth of businesses affected how resources were used and what motivated people to make economic choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Incentives, Choice, and Consumer Behavior (E.IC)
description_plain: Your child understands why people make certain choices as consumers and how incentives affect those choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the positive and negative effects of technology on labor.
description_plain: Your child can identify both the good and bad effects that technology has had on workers and jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of forced and exploitative labor systems in the economic development of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can explain how forced and unfair labor systems helped shape the economy of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explore varying viewpoints on the role and purpose of taxation and government spending historical and contemporary debate.
description_plain: Your child can talk about different opinions on why we pay taxes and how the government spends money, both in the past and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Micro and Macro Economics (E.MI)
description_plain: Your child understands basic ideas about how individuals, businesses, and the whole economy work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define and analyze the concept of “fair lending practices” and the history of discrimination and systemic inequalities in the US financial system.
description_plain: Your child can explain what fair lending means and how unfair treatment has happened in the U.S. financial system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how compound interest can generate both wealth and debt.
description_plain: Your child understands that compound interest can help people grow their money or make their debt bigger.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss how unexpected life situations can affect financial well-being and savings plans.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how unexpected events, like losing a job or a medical emergency, can change a family's savings and financial plans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the education and training requirements, income potential, and primary duties of at least two jobs available to high school students.
description_plain: Your child can compare at least two jobs that high school students can get, looking at what training they need, how much they can earn, and what the jobs involve.

subject: Social Studies
description: Earning, Saving, and Spending (E.ES)
description_plain: Your child understands how people earn, save, and spend money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Economics
description_plain: Your child understands basic ideas about how money, goods, and services work in our society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe examples of how conquest and colonialism in North America affected Indigenous peoples' identities, cultures, and communities.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how taking over land and colonization in North America changed the lives and cultures of Indigenous peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human Environmental Interaction (G.HE)
description_plain: Your child understands how people and the environment affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe how the physical and human characteristics of places and regions connect to human identities and cultures.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the features of a place, like its land and people, connect to the way people see themselves and their culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain examples of the influence of physical geography on human cultural elements such as law, religion, diet, and architecture.
description_plain: Your child can explain how things like land, climate, and geography have shaped laws, religions, foods, and buildings.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the effects of migration on immigrants and the culture where they settle.
description_plain: Your child can find out how moving to a new place affects immigrants and the culture of the area where they settle.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the causes and effects of migration, settlement, and cultural diffusion, in the expansion of the United States after the American Revolution until the end of the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can describe why people moved, settled, and shared cultures in the United States after the American Revolution up to the Civil War, and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Migration and Movement (G.MM)
description_plain: Your child understands why people move and how it changes them and the places they go.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret maps to identify the changes in political geography, population, and economic development of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to see how the United States has changed in its borders, population, and economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct and use maps and other representations, technologies, and spatial thinking to understand changes in the demographic composition of North America.
description_plain: Your child can make and use maps and other tools to understand how the people living in North America have changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geographic Reasoning (G.GR)
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps and other tools to study geography.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geography
description_plain: Your child understands the basics of geography, including places, people, and environments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the historical and contemporary means of attempting to create more inclusive societies, including the importance of advocacy and activism related to the expansion of justice, equality, and equity for historically underrepresented individuals and groups.
description_plain: Your child can talk about ways people have tried to make society fairer, including the importance of speaking up and taking action for justice and equality.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the methods of individuals and movements responsible for the expansion of justice, equality, equity, rights, and responsibilities of citizenship from historically underrepresented groups at the local and national levels.
description_plain: Your child can identify how people and groups have worked to expand rights and fairness for those who have been left out, both locally and nationally.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the evolution of political parties and interest groups and their effect on events, issues, and ideas.
description_plain: Your child can explain how political parties and interest groups have changed over time and affected important events and ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Engagement (C.CE)
description_plain: Your child understands how people get involved in their communities and government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze issues involving liberty in conflict with equality or authority, individual rights in conflict with the common good, or majority rule in conflict with minority rights.
description_plain: Your child can discuss situations where freedom, fairness, and authority can be in conflict, like when individual rights clash with what is best for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze important political and ethical values for individual rights and their lasting effect on the status, rights, and liberties of historically underrepresented individuals and groups embodied in documents such as the Declaration of Independence, the United States and Oregon Constitutions, and the Bill of Rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important documents, like the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution, protect rights and freedoms, especially for groups who have been treated unfairly.

subject: Social Studies
description: Democratic Principles (C.DP)
description_plain: Your child understands the main ideas and values behind democracy in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and understand how to apply the rights and responsibilities of individuals under the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can identify their rights and responsibilities under the Constitution and knows how to use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identity, Roles, and Responsibilities (C.IR)
description_plain: Your child understands what it means to be a citizen, including their roles and responsibilities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the interconnection of governing power and responsibility in the system of checks and balances.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the branches of government share power and keep each other in check.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the process of elections in the legislative and executive branches, and the process of nomination/confirmation of individuals in the judicial and executive branches.
description_plain: Your child can describe how elections work for lawmakers and leaders, and how judges and some officials are chosen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the United States republican form of government to direct democracy, theocracy, oligarchy, authoritarianism, and monarchy.
description_plain: Your child can compare the U.S. government to other types of governments, like monarchies or dictatorships.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate how the persistence of racism, sexism, and other forms of prejudice, affected laws, treaties, and Supreme Court decisions from 1787 to 1865 on sovereignty, status, rights, and liberties of historically underrepresented individuals and groups.
description_plain: Your child can explain how racism, sexism, and other prejudices have affected laws and court decisions about rights and freedoms from 1787 to 1865.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine and analyze significant primary source documents establishing, limiting, or denying civil rights in the United States and Oregon.
description_plain: Your child can look at important historical documents to see how they gave, limited, or took away people's rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the reasons for the failure of the Articles of Confederation and the adoption of the U.S. Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Articles of Confederation didn't work and why the U.S. Constitution was created.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the influence of the Enlightenment and Native American political ideas and documents on the American Revolution and the framework of the American government.
description_plain: Your child can describe how ideas from the Enlightenment and Native American governments influenced the American Revolution and the U.S. government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of the influence of the democratic political concepts of ancient Greece and Rome on the structure and ideas of the Founders and the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ancient Greece and Rome influenced the ideas and structure of the U.S. government.
